--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://media.market.us/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/prevalence-of-zoonotic-diseases-among-human-diseases-700x577.png
DESCRIPTION: A bar chart illustrates the worldwide prevalence of various zoonotic diseases among human diseases in 2023, highlighting rabies as the most common at 10%, followed by HIV/AIDS and malaria at 5%, with other diseases like Lyme disease, salmonellosis, dengue fever, tuberculosis, influenza, and Zika virus showing lower percentages.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://media.market.us/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/zoonotic-disease-distribution-700x537.png
DESCRIPTION: A pie chart displays the distribution of zoonotic diseases worldwide in 2023, showing the percentage of cases across different age groups: adults (56%), children (22%), and elderly (22%).

IMAGE 3:
URL: http://cdn.statcdn.com/Infographic/images/normal/30872.jpeg
DESCRIPTION: An infographic shows that the majority of emerging infectious diseases are zoonotic.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://media.market.us/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/gender-distribution-in-selected-zoonotic-diseases.png
DESCRIPTION: The chart displays the gender distribution percentages for several zoonotic diseases worldwide in 2023, highlighting differences between males and females in conditions such as HIV/AIDS, Lyme disease, rabies, COVID-19, and the overall prevalence of zoonotic diseases.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://media.market.us/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/modes-of-transmission-of-zoonotic-diseases-1024x786.png
DESCRIPTION: A pie chart illustrating the modes of transmission of zoonotic diseases worldwide in 2023, with the largest portion attributed to direct contact at 60%, followed by foodborne at 20%, vector-borne at 17%, and environmental exposure at 3%.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Approximately 60% of known infectious diseases are zoonotic, with around 200 documented globally, highlighting their significant presence in human health (Source 1, Source 2).
Emerging infectious diseases predominantly originate from animals, emphasizing the need for targeted surveillance in wildlife and livestock areas to prevent outbreaks (Source 4, Source 5).

Environmental changes like climate fluctuation influence disease spread, altering host habitats and increasing human-animal interactions, thereby heightening zoonotic disease risks (Source 3, Source 5).
